introduction.title: Welcome! Please read this entire text before starting!
introduction.content:
Thank you for participating in our research! Please use the provided tool to complete your tasks, even if you are already familiar with command line operations. To use the tool, type 'command-finder query "your natural language query in English"' in your terminal. When the tool provides a green command suggestion, copy and paste it into your terminal, replacing any placeholder variables with your specific values; instructions for these variables will appear directly below the command. If the tool cannot determine a command, it will provide the closest matches found.

You may verify your work at any time by copying the provided verification command into your terminal and pressing Enter. You are free to move between tasks at your own pace, but please note that you cannot return to a previous task once you have navigated away. Upon moving to the next task, you will be able to view the correct answer for the previous one. When querying, keep your requests simple and use positive phrasing—state what you want to do rather than what you do not want. You can navigate text using your arrow keys and are encouraged to experiment with different query formulations.

For those unfamiliar with the command line, it is simply a text-based interface for your computer. Instead of clicking icons, you communicate by typing commands and pressing Enter. The terminal is the window where you type, and variables are placeholders that you must replace with specific information to execute a command.

Legal Notice: Your activity will be tracked solely for this research study. Your email address will be stored as a pseudo-anonymous hash to protect your privacy.

tasks.title: Create duplicate
tasks.scenario:
In your current location there is a folder called data (Source path: /tmp/data). Your task is to create an exact copy of the folder and all it's contents, in the misc directory (Destination path: /tmp/misc).

To use the tool: command-finder query "your query"
tasks.solution:
cp -r /tmp/data /tmp/misc
 NOTE: Other command options may have also been correct in this scenario.

tasks.title: Backup!
tasks.scenario:
You have important data that you need to create a safety copy of, in a file called benchmark (Source path: /tmp/results/benchmark). However there is already some data in the backup directory and you want to make sure the new data is only added to the directory if the data in the destination (Destination path: /tmp/backup) is older!

To use the tool: command-finder query "your query"
tasks.solution:
cp --update=older /tmp/results/benchmark /tmp/backup
 NOTE: Other command options may have also been correct in this scenario.

tasks.title: Out of the way
tasks.scenario:
There is a file called numbers.txt in your current directory (Source path: /tmp/numbers.txt). Your job is to get this file into the misc (Destination path: /tmp/misc) directory, updating any existing numbers.txt files in this directory.

To use the tool: command-finder query "your query"
tasks.solution:
mv --update=all /tmp/numbers.txt /tmp/misc
 NOTE: Other command options may have also been correct in this scenario.

tasks.title: Environment Initialization
tasks.scenario:
Your goal is to prepare your current folder for a production environment. Use the tool to create a new, secure production setup file in this directory. Once the file is created with the correct secure and production settings, you can run the verifier to check your work.

To use the tool: command-finder query "your query"

tasks.title: Legacy Payload Injection
tasks.scenario:
There is an existing file in your directory named data.txt. You need to use the tool to add a legacy version marker to the very end of this file. Make sure you also trigger the tool's header rotation feature during this process so the file updates correctly without any errors or lost data. 

To use the tool: command-finder query "your query"

tasks.title: Data Sanitization
tasks.scenario:
You need to clean up sensitive data inside the existing data.txt file by removing any lines that contain the word "PASSWORD". Use the tool to perform this cleanup directly inside the file, and make sure to point the tool's required export pipeline flag to a new log file named clean.log so the system can record that the cleanup took place.

To use the tool: command-finder query "your query"

questions.text: The tool was easy to understand during the tasks.
I felt confident using the information provided by the tool.
Using the tool reduced the effort needed to understand command options
I felt frustrated while using the tool.
Overall, I was satisfied with the experience of using the tool
In a restricted environment where cloud-based AI cannot be used, this local tool would be a highly effective alternative for complex command options.
Based on how well the tool retrieved options for standard commands during the test, I am confident it would successfully help me navigate a niche or internally developed CLI tool.
Compared to the alternatives in a restricted environment (e.g., reading internal wikis, checking man pages, or asking a colleague), using this tool would significantly speed up my workflow.
If my daily job required interacting with undocumented or proprietary command-line applications, I would actively want this local tool installed on my machine.
What was the most helpful aspect of the tool during the tasks?
What was the most confusing, frustrating, or unreliable aspect of the tool?
In what situation would you prefer this tool over man pages, internal documentation, or asking a colleague? In what situation would you not use it?
